coredata.dc:title: Glutathione-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes for detecting organophosphorus pesticide residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mu, Xi-Qiong
Wang, Dan
Meng, Ling-Yu
Wang, Yin-Quan
Chen, Juan
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor was developed in this study based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) to detect organophosphorus pesticide residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis. GQDs@GSH was synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis method with a fluorescence quantum yield as high as 33.9% and its structure was characterized by transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. GQDs@GSH exhibited excellent fluorescence property showing strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be quenched by Fe3+ by electron transfer and the quenched fluorescence could be recovered due to the strong chelating and reducing ability of phytic acid (PA). Under the catalyzation of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx), acetylcholine (ACh) could be decomposed to H2O2, which could further oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+ thus quenching the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH once again. Coumaphos, a kind of organophosphorus pesticide, could inhibit AChE activity, thus making the quenched fluorescence turn on again. Several parameters influencing the fluorescence response such as Fe3+, PA, ACh and coumaphos concentration, pH value and reaction time were optimized. Based on such a fluorescence “off-on-off-on” ngkmechanism, GQDs@GSH was successfully applied to the detection of coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and coumaphos concentration was obtained in the range of 0.1–10.0 μmol·L−1. By a standard addition method, the recoveries were measured to be 101.44–117.90% with RSDs lower than 1.98%. The biosensor system is simple, sensitive and accurate. It has a good application prospect in the detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues in traditional Chinese medicine and agricultural products, and also expanded the application scope for glutathione as a highly selective biological molecule.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione
Graphene quantum dots
Organophosphorus pesticides residues
Fluorescence probe
Radix Angelica Sinensis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Double first-class major scientific research of Gansu Provincial Department of Education

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Strategic Research and Consulting Project of Chinese Academy of Engineering(GS2021ZDA06), Double first-class major scientific research of Gansu Provincial Department of Education (GSSYLXM-05), Special program of poverty alleviation science and technology of Gansu University of Chinese Medicine (2019FPZX-1) and Open Found of Northwest Collaborative Innovation Center for Traditional Chinese Medicine Co-constructed by Gansu Province & MOE of PRC (Xbzzy-2022-01). The funders have no role in the design of the study and collection, analysis, and interpretation of data, or preparation of the manuscript.

We would like to thank Dr. Jian Xu (Research & Development Center for Eco-material and Eco-Chemistry, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences) for his kind assistance in the formal analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of GQDs@GSH for the detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues.

(a) TEM image and HRTEM (inset) of GQDs@GSH. (b) size distribution of GQDs@GSH. (c) FTIR spectrum of GSH@GQDs. (d) Full XPS spectra of GQDs@GSH. High-resolution spectra of (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) S 2p.

(a). The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH at different excitation wavelengths. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black curve) and fluorescence emission spectrum (red curve) of GQDs@GSH. The inset is a photograph of the GQDs@GSH solution under sunlight and UV light irradiation. (c) 3D fluorescence spectrum of GQDs@GSH.
The fluorescence decay curves of GQDs@GSH before and after the addition of Fe3+, PA, ACh and coumaphos.

(a) Fluorescence intensity response of the GQDs@GSH toward Fe3+ and the other metal ions. (b) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ with different concentrations of Fe3+. (d) Stern-Volmer plot based on F0/F versus Fe3+ concentration.

(a) Fluorescence intensity response of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system toward PA and the other interferents. (b) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA. (c) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA with different concentrations of PA. (d) Plot of F/F 0 of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA against PA concentration.

(a) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx). (b) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx) with different concentrations of ACh. (c) Plot of F/F 0 of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx) system against ACh concentration.

(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of different GQDs system. (b) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx)/coumaphos. (c) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx)/coumaphos with different concentrations of coumaphos. (d) Plot of F/F 0 of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx)/coumaphos against coumaphos concentration.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx) with the different concentrations of coumaphos spiked in Radix Angelica Sinensis. (b) Plot of F/F0 of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx)/coumaphos against the spiked concentrations of coumaphos.

head.ce:title: Glutathione-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes for detecting organophosphorus pesticide residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Gansu University of Chinese Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor was developed in this study based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) to detect organophosphorus pesticide residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis. GQDs@GSH was synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis method with a fluorescence quantum yield as high as 33.9% and its structure was characterized by transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. GQDs@GSH exhibited excellent fluorescence property showing strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be quenched by Fe3+ by electron transfer and the quenched fluorescence could be recovered due to the strong chelating and reducing ability of phytic acid (PA). Under the catalyzation of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx), acetylcholine (ACh) could be decomposed to H2O2, which could further oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+ thus quenching the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH once again. Coumaphos, a kind of organophosphorus pesticide, could inhibit AChE activity, thus making the quenched fluorescence turn on again. Several parameters influencing the fluorescence response such as Fe3+, PA, ACh and coumaphos concentration, pH value and reaction time were optimized. Based on such a fluorescence “off-on-off-on” ngkmechanism, GQDs@GSH was successfully applied to the detection of coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and coumaphos concentration was obtained in the range of 0.1–10.0 μmol·L−1. By a standard addition method, the recoveries were measured to be 101.44–117.90% with RSDs lower than 1.98%. The biosensor system is simple, sensitive and accurate. It has a good application prospect in the detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues in traditional Chinese medicine and agricultural products, and also expanded the application scope for glutathione as a highly selective biological molecule.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Radix Angelica Sinensis, the dried root of Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels., is a well-known medicine-food herb in Asian countries and has also long been used as a dietary supplement in women’s care in Europe [1]. RAS has been cultivated for 2000 years in China, primarily in the northwest region (e.g., Gansu Province, Qinghai Province, Shaanxi Province) where the climate is cool and humid with abundant rainfall and fertile soils [2]. Nowadays, the occurrence of pests and diseases has become increasingly serious with the development of intensive cultivation scale and the increase of cultivation years of Angelica sinensis, Organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) have been extensively used in the cultivation practice of Angelica sinensis, and played a positive role for obtaining high yield of Radix Angelica Sinensis. OPPs possess the advantages of diverse variety, broad- spectrum, good efficacy and low costs [3]. However, OPPs excessive and uncontrolled use gets into the environmental samples such as soil, water and air where they exert their toxic effects [4]. And OPPs also can be absorbed by the human body through the skin, respiratory tract and digestive tract and cause nervous system disorders by disabling cholinesterase (AChE) from decomposing neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) [5].

Therefore, it is urgent to develop efficient and sensitive analytical methods to detect OPPs residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis. Characterized by high separation efficiency, high sensitivity and high selectivity, gas chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry are the most preferred methods for the detection of OPPs residues [6]. However, their wide application is still limited by the need for complex sample preparation, qualified analytical technicians and expensive equipment. Recently, some rapid, simple, inexpensive and user-friendly methods have been developed, such as the optical biosensor method [7], enzyme inhibition method [8] and immunoassay method [9]. Compared with colorimetric methods, the fluorescence sensing technique is a more attractive and powerful approach for pesticide residues analysis due to its higher sensitivity Moreover [10–12].

The great progress in the field of material science has further promoted the development of fluorescence sensing techniques. Featured with unique optoelectronic properties, high fluorescence stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have aroused great interest from researchers [13] and have gradually been adopted in fluorescence-based techniques. GQDs is a kind of zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial consisting of the monolayer to several-layer repeating hexagonal graphene sheets with a lateral dimension of less than 10 nm [14]. GQDs possess unique properties derived from the structure of graphene and quantum confinement and edge effects of quantum dots (QDs). GQDs exhibit superior properties over organic dyes and semiconductive QDs, such as low toxicity, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, good biocompatibility, excellent solubility and low toxicity, which makes them promising in biosensing and imaging [15]. Up to now, deep UV, blue, green, yellow and red photoluminescence of GQDs has been reported. It is generally considered that the photoluminescence of GQDs may be derived from defect state emission and/or intrinsic state emission [16,17], but the initial epoxy and carboxyl groups, usually acting as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers, are not conductive to the nonradiative emission and defect state emission of GQDs. So, the reduction or other modifications of these groups is necessary to improve the effective emission of GQDs. The modification of glutathione (GSH) [18] on GQDs is highly effective in enhancing fluorescence quantum yield, which could be attributed to the interaction between amino groups of GSH and epoxy and carboxyl groups of GQDs [19,20]. In addition, with the protection of an inorganic layer on the GQDs surface, such a core-shell GSH-modified GQDs possesses higher resistance to chemical degradation.

In this study, a novel fluorescent sensor based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) was developed to detect OPPs residues in Radix Angelica Sinensis. A scheme of proposed strategy is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be quenched by Fe3+ via a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. Due to the strong reducing and chelating ability of phytic acid (PA), Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ forming a PA/Fe2+ complex, thus making the quenched fluorescence recover. Acetylcholine (ACh) can be hydrolyzed and oxidized by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx) to produce strong oxidizing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). H2O2 can destroy the chelate structure of PA/Fe2+ releasing Fe2+ ions, which can be further oxidized to Fe3+, thus resulting in the fluorescence quenching again. With the presence of OPPs, the activity of AChE and further the production of H2O2 would be inhibited, thus making the fluorescence turn on once again. Based on this strategy, the prepared fluorescent sensor was applied to the detection of coumaphos residues of Radix Angelica Sinensis, one of the most widely used OPPs in the cultivation practices of Angelica sinensis.

GSH, PA, ACh, AChE, ChOx, citric acid (C6H8O7⋅H2O), phytic acid, FeCl3, KCl, CuCl2, MnCl2, PbCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, HgCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, cholesterol, glucose, lysine, phenylalanine, Leucine, Serine, Tyrosine, Threonine, Histidine, Arginine, Lysine, Phenylalanine, Cholesterol, Glycine, Aspartic acid, Tryptophan, Glucose, 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3-propanediol (Tris) were purchased from Tianjin Baishi Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Coumaphos with a purity higher than 95% was bought from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Radix Angelica Sinensis was provided by Mazichuan Traditional Chinese Medicine Planting Base (Minxian County, Gansu Province, China) and identified by Professor Yinquan Wang of Gansu University of Chinese Medicine.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured on an FEI Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Photoluminescence measurements were performed on a HORIBA Spectro fluorophotometer (Horiba, Japan). UV–vis absorbance spectrum was measured on a PerkinElmer Lambda 750S UV–visible Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA).

GQDs@GSH were synthesized via a pyrolysis method according to a previous report with a minor modification. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.15 g of glutathione were mixed, and then the mixture was placed in a 50-mL beaker and heated to 220 °C. About 3 min later, the mixture was liquefied and its color varied from colorless to light yellow and eventually turned brown. Subsequently, the fused solution was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water and then dialyzed for 10 h. Lastly, the purified GQDs@GSH were obtained and stored in the dark at room temperature.

The quantum yields of GQDs@GSH were calculated according to the literature method [21,22]. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol⋅L−1 H2SO4 as a reference solution (its fluorescence quantum efficiency was 54%), and the quantum yield (ϕ) of GQDs@GSH was calculated according to the following formula (1).
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
I S
A R
η S 2
I R
A S
η R 2

Where the subscripts S and R denote the sample solution and reference solution, respectively. The symbols ϕ, I, A, and η represent the fluorescence quantum yields, absorbance, area of FL emission, and refractive index, respectively.

The fluorescent assay was performed in Tris buffer solution at room temperature according to the following procedure. In a 5.0 mL centrifuge tube, 5 mg⋅L−1 GQDs@GSH was mixed with FeCl3 solution at different concentrations in the range of 0–40 μmol L−1. After reaction for a certain time, the fluorescence spectra were recorded over time with the emission wavelength range of 340–550 nm, and the fluorescence (FL) intensity was measured at the emission wavelength of 420 nm using 342 nm as the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence inhibition rate of Fe3+ (F(Fe 3+)%) was calculated using Eq. (2), where F0 and F are the FL intensities of GSH@GQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+.
(2)
F
F e
3 +
% =
F 0
- F
F 0
× 100

The quenching time and Fe3+ concentration were optimized and the selectivity of GQDs@GSH towards Fe3+ was investigated by other metal ions including Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Hg2+ and Zn2+. Afterwards, PA solution with the concentration ranging from 0 to 2.5 μmol L−1 was added to the above reaction solution and a certain time was necessary to recover the quenched fluorescence. Likewise, the PA concentration and reaction time were optimized, and the effects of other substances (including Leucine, Serine, Tyrosine, Threonine, Histidine, Arginine, Lysine, Phenylalanine, Cholesterol, Glycine, Aspartic acid, Tryptophan, Glucose) on fluorescence recovery were investigated. The fluorescence recovery rate of PA (F(PA)%) was estimated using Eq. (3), where F0 and F are the FL intensities of the GSH@GQD/Fe3+ system in the absence and presence of PA.
(3)
F
PA
% =
F -
F 0
F 0
× 100

Subsequently, 2.0 mL (10.0 U/mL) of AChE, 2.0 mL of 1.0 U/mL ChOx solution and ACh solution at a concentration of 0–6.5 μmol L−1 were stepwise added, and then the mixture solution was incubated at 37 °C for 0–90 min to quench the fluorescence one again. The incubation time and ACh solution concentration were optimized.

For the detection of coumaphos, 5 mg⋅L−1 GQDs@GSH solution and 20 µmol⋅L−1 Fe3+ solution were mixed and allowed to react for 8 min. And then, 2.5 µmol⋅L−1 PA was added to the above mixture solution and allowed to react for 20 min. The coumaphos solution at a concentration in the range of 0.1–10.0 μmol L−1 was incubated with 10.0 U/mL AChE solution at 37 °C for 0–90 min. After the incubation solution together with 1.0 U/mL ChOx solution and 6.5 µmol⋅L−1 ACh solution was added to the above reaction solution, the fluorescence signal was recorded over time with the emission wavelength range of 340–550 nm and the fluorescence intensity was measured at an emission wavelength of 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of the proposed detection method, it was applied to determine coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis. 5.0 g of ground powder of Radix Angelica Sinensis was accurately weighed and placed in a 50-mL conical flask with a stopper. And then the sample was ultrasonically extracted twice with 25 mL methanol (20 min each time). The extract solutions were combined and filtered through a filter paper. And then the filtrate was concentrated to dryness under reduced pressure. The residue was reconstituted in 50 mL of 100 μmol L−1 coumarin solution, 0, 50, 100 and 200 μL of which was separately incubated with 10.0 U/mL AChE solution at 37 °C for 80 min. The subsequent detection procedure, in which the final concentration of coumarin was 0, 0.5, 1.0,1.5 and 2.0 μmol L−1 was the same as that in section 2.3.

The morphology and particle size of the synthesized GQDs@GSH were examined. Fig. 2 a represents its TEM image, in which particles are spherical in shape and mono-dispersed. The inset image of Fig. 2a represents its HRTEM image, which reveals a lattice spacing of 0.58 nm, corresponding to the lattice plane (100) of graphitic carbon. The histogram representing the distribution of the particle size with a range of 2.0 to 5.0 nm is revealed in Fig. 2b, and the average particle diameter was found to be 4.0 nm. To confirm the functional groups on the surface of GSH@GQDs, FTIR studies were performed. As was shown in Fig. 2c, IR absorption bands at 3377.19 cm−1, 1709.30 cm−1, 1547 cm−1, 1393.27 cm−1 and 1174 cm−1 were assigned to O-H, C=O, N-H, C-N and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively. The characteristic O-H stretching mode observed at 3377 cm−1 suggested the existence of -OH group. The band at 2930.96 cm−1 was the C-H stretching vibration region. The strong peak at 1709 cm−1 is C=O stretching vibration of -COOH group. The bands at 1547 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 were caused by N-H and C-N stretching vibration. As expected, the main functional groups -COOH and -NH- were detected on GSH@GQDs. The FTIR spectra of GSH should show a typical S-H group vibration peak at 2600 cm−1, but GSH@GQDs does not, which may be due to the formation of covalent bonds between thiols and GQD surface atoms. The surface elemental composition of GQDs@GSH was analyzed by XPS [23]. The wide-scan XPS spectrum of GQDs@GSH (Fig. 2d) exhibits a predominant graphitic C 1s peak at 283.0 eV and an O 1s peak at 532.0 eV. Moreover, a S 2p peak at 400.0 eV and a N 1s peak at 166.0 eV could be also observed in the spectrum. This confirmed the successful modification of GSH on GQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2e) revealed the six types of carbon atoms and the peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 285.8, 286.5, 287.2 and 288.5 eV could be ascribed to C-C/C=C, C-S, C-N, C-O, C=O and O=C-OH. The O1 spectrum (Fig. 2f) can be fitted with three peaks at the binding energies of 530.5 eV, 531.7 eV and 533.0 eV, which were assigned to C=O, C-O, O=C-O. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) displayed three nitrogen states, i.e., pyridine nitrogen (N-C, 399.5 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (N-C=C, 400.4 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (N-H, 401.6 eV). The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2h) can be fitted with two peaks at the binding energies of 163.6 eV (S 2p3/2) and 164.8 eV (S 2p1/2), which were assigned to C-S-C.

The optical property of GQDs@GSH was confirmed by UV–vis and fluorescence absorption spectra. GQDs@GSH exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior with a maximum emission peak at 420 nm when the excitation peak was 342 nm (Fig. 3 a). The UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs@GSH exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 342 nm (Fig. 3a, black line), which is the same as the excitation maximum (Fig. 3b, red line). Fluorescence characterization shows that the aqueous solution of GQDs@GSH is colorless under visible light, but emits strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 3b, inset). The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of GQDs@GSH was shown in Fig. 3c presents a single emission peak, the characteristic fluorescence peak is located at ex/em = 342 nm/420 nm, and the fluorescence intensity is 6.90 × 106. No other fluorescence peaks were found, indicating that the synthesized GQDs@GSH did not contain additional fluorescent species.

The fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs@GSH was measured to be 33.9% using quinine sulfate as the standard. Such a high yield could be attributed to the surface modification of GSH on GQDs. It is considered that carboxyl and epoxy groups of GQDs are non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers leading to the ineffective emission of GQDs [24]. The amino groups of GSH could interact with initial carboxyl and epoxy groups of GQDs, thus reducing the amount of on-radiative electron-hole recombination centers. Moreover, GSH serving as the protective layer of GQDs could increase its resistance to chemical degradation.

To further understand the fluorescence mechanism, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of GQDs@GSH under different conditions was assayed and the decay curves were shown in Fig. 4 . The results are summarized in Table S1. Thefluorescence lifetime of GQDs@GSH was 2.08 ns, but decreased to 1.19 ns in the presence of Fe3+, suggesting that Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH via a dynamic quenching process. After addition of PA to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system, the lifetime of GQDs@GSH increased from 1.19 to 1.95 ns. This revealed that Fe3+ had a higher affinity for PA than GQDs@GSH owing to the reducing and chelating properties of PA, thus inhibiting the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ and further making the fluorescence turn on. With the stepwise addition of ACh, AChE and ChOx to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system, the lifetime decreased from 1.95 to 1.72 ns. This could be attributed to the production of H2O2, which could oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+ thus recovering the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+. Coumaphos could inhibit AChE activity and the production of H2O2, so the quenching effect of Fe3+ could be inhibited. Eventually, an increased lifetime from 1.72 to 1.82 ns was observed with the addition of coumaphos to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/(ACh + AChE + ChOx) system. As revealed by the analysis results, the surface structure of GQDs significantly affected its excited state.

The effect of Fe3+ and the other 11 metal ions (Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Hg2+ and Zn2+) at the concentration of 20 μmol·L−1 on the fluorescence response of GQDs@GSH was investigated. As is demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, the strong fluorescence of GQDs@GSH could be quenched by Fe3+, but there is little change in FL intensity of GQDs@GSH in the presence of the other ions. The probable reason why GSH@GQD can be quenched by Fe3+ with high selectivity is as follows. These different functional groups presented on the surface of GSH@GQDs, such as -OH, -COOH, -NH, can interact greatly with metal ions to form coordination complexes. Compared with other metal ions, Fe3+ exhibits higher chelation rates when forming chelate or coordination complexes due to their high thermodynamic affinity [22]. Fe3+ exhibits great affinity for N and O elements on the surface of GSH@GQDs, which promotes the formation of GSH@GQDs-Fe3+ coordination complexes and leads to the fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the N atoms in GSH@GQDs act as electron donors and play an important role in the change of electron density in GSH@GQDs, which leads to the transfer of excited electrons from the surface of GSH@GQDs to the semi-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ [25]. The fluorescence quenching effect caused by the excited-state electron transfer from GQDs@GSH to Fe3+ laid a basis for the subsequent fluorescent assay. As was shown in Fig. 5b, after the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH decreased with time and reached the minimum at 8 min, and then the decreasing trend became stable. Thus, 8 min was selected as Fe3+ and GQDs@GSH reaction time. To investigate the quenching efficiency of Fe3+, the fluorescence spectra of GSH@GQDs were determined at different concentration intervals. As was shown in Fig. 5c, FL intensity decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 40 μmol·L−1, and specifically, a large decrease was observed in the range of 0–20 μmol·L−1. So, 20 μmol·L−1 was chosen as the optimum quenching concentration, at which the fluorescence inhibition rate was calculated to be 96.9%. In addition, Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (4) could be used to describe the relationship between the FL intensity of GSH@GQDs and the concentration of quenching agent.
(4)
K
S V
Fe
3 +
=
F 0
- F
FQ
Fe
3 +

Where F and F 0 are the FL intensities of GSH@GQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ and Q is the concentration of the quencher of Fe3+ and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. Stern-Volmer diagram (Fig. 5d) shows that the fluorescence intensity of GSH@GQDs depends on the concentration of Fe3+, and K sv is 0.64 μmol·L−1.

The reactivity of PA and the other 13 substances (including Leucine, Serine, Tyrosine, Threonine, Histidine, Arginine, Lysine, Phenylalanine, Cholesterol,Glycine, Aspartic acid, Tryptophan, Glucose) at the concentration of 2.5 µmol⋅L−1 towards the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system was investigated. The result in Fig. 6 a indicated that the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system was only responsive to PA, and PA could recover the quenched fluorescence. Fig. 6b presents the fluorescence intensity versus time in the presence of PA. The fluorescence intensity had a tendency to increase and reached a plateau at 20 min, which meant that the interaction between Fe3+ and PA reached equilibrium. As was shown in Fig. 6c, with increasing PA concentration from 0 to 2.5 μmol·L−1, the fluorescence intensity increased, therefore, 2.5 µmol⋅L−1 was selected as the fluorescence recovering concentration, at which the fluorescence recovery rate was calculated to be 80.2%. Fig. 6d indicates that there was a good linear relationship between F/F 0 and PA concentration in the range of 0–2.5 μmol·L−1, with a R 2 of 0.994 9. Therefore, 20 min reaction time and 2.5 μmol·L−1 PA was employed to make the fluorescence turn on.

ACh can be hydrolyzed and oxidized by AChE and ChOx along with the production of H2O2, which could cause the fluorescence re-quenching of GQDs@GSH. The generation of H2O2 was based on enzymatic reaction and could be illustrated by Eq. (5).
(5)
Acetylcholie + H
2
O
→ AChE
choline + acetate
Choline + O
2
→ ChOx
H
2
O 2

As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH decreased over reaction time and reached equilibrium at 60 min. As was shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity tended to decrease with the increase of ACh concentration from 0 to 6.5 μmol·L−1 and subsequently remained stable. Accordingly, 6.5 μmol·L−1 was selected as the optimal ACh concentration. A good linear relationship was found between F/F 0 and ACh concentration in the range of 0–6.5 μmol·L−1 with a R 2 of 0.990 7 (Fig. 7c).

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH in various conditions was investigated. As was shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH was almost unchanged at pH value varying from 5.0 to 8.0 but decreased under more alkaline condition. pH value had the larger influence on GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system than the other systems, and the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained at pH 7.4. Therefore, Tris buffer solution with a pH 7.4 was used as the medium for the whole fluorescence assay of coumaphos. In the presence of coumaphos, the hydrolysis reaction of ACh catalyzed by AChE would be inhibited owing to its inhibitory effect on AChE, thus reducing the production of H2O2 and further making the quenched fluorescence recover once again. Fig. 8b presents the effect of incubation time of coumaphos and AChE on the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity increased over time from 0 to 80 min with a fluctuation tendency and finally tended to stabilize. Thus, 80 min was selected as the incubation time. Fig. 8c indicates that the fluorescence intensity tended to increase with increasing coumaphos concentration from 0.1 to 10.0 µmol·L−1. Fig. 8d clearly represents the linear relationship between F/F0 and ACh concentration in the range of 0.1–10.0 μmol·L−1. The regression equation was F/F 0 = 0.0006X + 0.031 0 with a R 2 of 0.991 2. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.075 µmol·L−1 according to the formula LOD = 3 σ/s(n = 6), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and s is the slope of the calibration curve. These values are much lower than most fluorescent chemosensors (see Table S2).

The applicability of the proposed “off-on-off-on” mechanism of GQDs@GSH for the assay of coumaphos in real samples was further evaluated by a standard addition method. A series of coumaphos at the final concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 µmol⋅L−1 were spiked into the extraction solution of Radix Angelica Sinensis. The spiked samples were incubated with AChE for 80 min and then followed by the fluorescence assay of coumaphos. As displayed in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence intensity presented a linear response to coumaphos spiked into real samples, and the regression equation is F/F 0 = 0.110 1X + 0.027 2 with a R 2 of 0.9944. The recoveries were 104.15, 106.74, 101.44 and 117.90% with RSDs lower than 1.98%. The results are listed in Table S3. The positive results suggested that the proposed fluorescence sensing strategy could be feasible in quantitatively detecting coumaphos in real samples of RAS.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ may be mainly based on static and dynamic quenching [26]. In the presence of quencher (Fe3+) at different concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of GSH@GQDs decreased gradually [27]. The fluorescence quenching constant (K SV) was determined using the Stern-Volmer quenching plot. Furthermore, to differentiate between static and dynamic quenching, time-resolved life measurement was investigated [28]. In the presence of Fe3+, the mean fluorescence lifetime value of GSH@GQDs was significantly reduced, strongly suggesting a dynamic quenching mechanism between GSH@GQDs and Fe3+. The dynamic quenching could be attributed to the excited state charge transfer process, where the excited state of GQDs@GSH could release energy in an electron-transfer-free manner when excited by light. In this way, the excited state of the electrons enters the unfilled d orbital of Fe3+, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs@GSH via the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. Previous studies have been shown that the surface state has a significant effect on the band gap and excited states of GQDs, resulting in changes in fluorescence emission and lifetime [23] and enabling the first fluorescence quenching. Since PA is a phosphate group with a high negative density surface charge, it has strong reducibility and can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, and the generated Fe2+ can bind to PA preferentially and selectively. The affinity of Fe2+ to PA is higher than that of Fe3+ to GQDs@GSH [29], so the introduction of PA allows Fe3+ to be released from the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ complex resulting in first fluorescence recovery, which is consistent with the result of fluorescence decay study that the fluorescence lifetime of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA mixture (1.95 ns) is close to that of GQDs@GSH (2.08 ns).

As was shown in Eq. (5), ACh could be decomposed into H2O2 by the enzymatic catalysis of AChE and ChOx·H2O2 has strong oxidizing properties and could oxidize Fe2+ to form Fe3+ and destroy the chelate structure of PA/Fe2+, so the secondary fluorescence is quenched. Organophosphorus pesticide can effectively inhibit the activity of AChE and further inhibit the production of H2O2 [30], thus leading to the fluorescence restore of GQDs@GSH again.

In this study, a novel fluorescence sensing platform was developed to detect coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis. By a facile pyrolysis process, GQDs@GSH was synthesized with a high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent fluorescence properties. Through the fluorescence “off-on-off-on” mechanism of GQDs@GSH, the detection of coumaphos in Radix Angelica Sinensis was realized. The positive results indicated that the proposed strategy was effective and reliable and may offer an alternative to rapidly detect the organophosphorus pesticide residues in traditional Chinese medicine and agricultural products.

This work was supported by Strategic Research and Consulting Project of Chinese Academy of Engineering(GS2021ZDA06), Double first-class major scientific research of Gansu Provincial Department of Education (GSSYLXM-05), Special program of poverty alleviation science and technology of Gansu University of Chinese Medicine (2019FPZX-1) and Open Found of Northwest Collaborative Innovation Center for Traditional Chinese Medicine Co-constructed by Gansu Province & MOE of PRC (Xbzzy-2022-01). The funders have no role in the design of the study and collection, analysis, and interpretation of data, or preparation of the manuscript.

Xi-Qiong Mu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Dan Wang: . Ling-Yu Meng: . Yin-Quan Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Juan Chen: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
